The year gui-wei likely corresponds to 443 or 503.
According to one prevailing opinion, the prince Wooto can be identified as the prince Oohodo, a grandson of [[Emperor Ōjin]] and a brother of Oshisaka-no-Oonakatsuhime ([[Emperor Ingyō|Emperor Ingyō's]] consort). Another theory holds that Wooto is Ōdo-no-Ookimi ([[Emperor Keitai]]). He may be a great-grandson (or a younger brother) of the prince Oohodo. If the gui-wei year corresponds to 503, Shima is presumed [[King Muryeong of Baekje]], whose name is recorded on [[Tomb of King Muryeong|his tomb]] as {{lang|lzh|斯摩}}: pronounced ''Sama'' in Korean, and ''Shima'' in Japanese.
On the basis of this ancient inscription, Korean scholar Kim Woon-Hoe theorizes a fraternal relationship between [[Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai of Japan]] and [[King Muryeong of Baekje]].<ref name=kim>[http://www.pressian.com/article/article.asp?article_num=40081126150215&Section=04 야마토 왕조의 중시조, 게이타이천황은 무령왕의 아우] ''Pressian'' (Seoul). December 5, 2008.</ref> A mirror was excavated from the [[tomb of King Muryeong]]. It is similar to the mirrors of [[Emperor Nintoku]] and [[Emperor Keitai]]. In the ancient [[Buyeo kingdom]], the bronze mirror represented the king.
== Earliest record == [[File:Depiction of the Suda Hachiman Shrine mirror in the Kii no kuni meisho zue.jpg|border|thumb|425x425px|Ink painting of the Suda Hachiman Shrine mirror as depicted in the Kii no kuni meisho zue]] The earliest record of this mirror is from the 3rd volume of the Kii no kuni meisho zue published in 1838. According to this text, the mirror had been passed down and treasured at the Suda Hachiman shrine. The monks of the shrine told the traditional account of this mirror's history. This account states that this mirror was presented to [[Empress Jingū]], after her conquest of the [[Samhan|Samhan confederacies]] of Korea by "the people of that land". Although there is doubt that [[Empress Jingū#Korean Invasion|her invasion of Korea]] was a historical event. The text mentions other theories for the historical origin of the mirror, and it makes an attempt to date the artifact by analysing the script used for the inscription. The text also contains an ink painting of the mirror.
==See also== * [[Seven-Branched Sword]]
==Notes== <references/>
==References== * {{cite book | last = Seeley | first = Christopher | title = A history of writing in Japan | publisher = University of Hawai'i Press | date = 2000 | isbn = 0-8248-2217-X }}
]]
{{Japan-art-stub}}

{{Infobox Historic Site | name = Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror | native_name = 隅田八幡神社人物画像鏡 | native_language = Japanese | image = Sumida Hatiman Mirror.JPG | caption = Bronze mirror of Suda Hachiman Shrine | locmapin = | location = [[Hashimoto, Wakayama]], [[Japan]] | area = | built = | architect = | architecture = | governing_body = | designation1 = Japan | designation1_offname = | designation1_date = | designation1_number = }} The {{Nihongo|'''Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror'''|隅田八幡神社人物画像鏡|Suda Hachiman Jinja Jinbutsu Gazō-kyō|lead=yes}} in [[Hashimoto, Wakayama]], [[Japan]] is a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure of Japan]]. It is a [[bronze mirror]] cast with 48 Chinese characters around the fifth century. It is an important artifact for the research in Japanese archaeology and [[Old Japanese]] language. It was found at [[Suda Hachiman Shrine]], and its earliest mention in text is in the 3rd volume of the Kii no kuni meisho zue (紀伊国名所図会; "Illustrated Guide to Places of Note in Kii Province") published in 1838.
==Inscriptions== The mirror says {{quote|{{lang|lzh|癸未年八月日十大王年男弟王在意柴沙加宮時斯麻念長寿遣開中費直穢人今州利二人等取白上同二百旱作此竟}}}} There are a few unclear points, but a tentative translation is "In the eighth month of a ''gui-wei'' year, in the reign of the great king, when the prince Wooto was at the [[wiktionary:忍坂|Osisaka]] Palace, Sima, wishing for longevity, sent two persons to make this mirror from 200 han of brand-new and fine bronze." The year gui-wei likely corresponds to 443 or 503.
According to one prevailing opinion, the prince Wooto can be identified as the prince Oohodo, a grandson of [[Emperor Ōjin]] and a brother of Oshisaka-no-Oonakatsuhime ([[Emperor Ingyō|Emperor Ingyō's]] consort). Another theory holds that Wooto is Ōdo-no-Ookimi ([[Emperor Keitai]]). He may be a great-grandson (or a younger brother) of the prince Oohodo. If the gui-wei year corresponds to 503, Shima is presumed [[King Muryeong of Baekje]], whose name is recorded on [[Tomb of King Muryeong|his tomb]] as {{lang|lzh|斯摩}}: pronounced ''Sama'' in Korean, and ''Shima'' in Japanese.
On the basis of this ancient inscription, Korean scholar Kim Woon-Hoe theorizes a fraternal relationship between [[Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai of Japan]] and [[King Muryeong of Baekje]].<ref name=kim>[http://www.pressian.com/article/article.asp?article_num=40081126150215&Section=04 야마토 왕조의 중시조, 게이타이천황은 무령왕의 아우] ''Pressian'' (Seoul). December 5, 2008.</ref> A mirror was excavated from the [[tomb of King Muryeong]]. It is similar to the mirrors of [[Emperor Nintoku]] and [[Emperor Keitai]]. In the ancient [[Buyeo kingdom]], the bronze mirror represented the king.
== Earliest record == [[File:Depiction of the Suda Hachiman Shrine mirror in the Kii no kuni meisho zue.jpg|border|thumb|425x425px|Ink painting of the Suda Hachiman Shrine mirror as depicted in the Kii no kuni meisho zue]] The earliest record of this mirror is from the 3rd volume of the Kii no kuni meisho zue published in 1838. According to this text, the mirror had been passed down and treasured at the Suda Hachiman shrine. The monks of the shrine told the traditional account of this mirror's history. This account states that this mirror was presented to [[Empress Jingū]], after her conquest of the [[Samhan|Samhan confederacies]] of Korea by "the people of that land". Although there is doubt that [[Empress Jingū#Korean Invasion|her invasion of Korea]] was a historical event. The text mentions other theories for the historical origin of the mirror, and it makes an attempt to date the artifact by analysing the script used for the inscription. The text also contains an ink painting of the mirror.

revision.text:
[[ファイル:Sumida Hatiman Mirror.JPG|thumb|right|隅田八幡神社人物画像鏡]] [[ファイル:隅田八幡神社人物画像鏡 (複製).JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|複製鏡}}{{small|[[和歌山県立紀伊風土記の丘]]企画展示時に撮影。}}]] '''隅田八幡神社人物画像鏡'''（すだはちまんじんじゃじんぶつがぞうきょう）は、[[和歌山県]][[橋本市]]の[[隅田八幡神社]]が所蔵する、[[5世紀]]から[[6世紀]]頃製作の[[銅鏡]]。鏡背の48字の[[金石文]]は、日本古代史、考古学、日本語学における貴重な資料である。[[国宝]]に指定されている<ref>国宝指定名称は「人物画'''象'''鏡」である。鏡は[[東京国立博物館]]に寄託されている。</ref>。
== 概要 == [[日本語]]の[[固有名詞]]が日本において[[漢字]]を用いて表記された最初期の例の一つ<ref>{{cite book|title=かな : その成立と変遷|author=小松茂美|publisher=岩波書店|year=1968|pages=14～20|doi=10.11501/2516831}}</ref>。
古代日本において[[大王 (ヤマト王権)|大王]]号を記す[[金石文]]としては[[鉄剣・鉄刀銘文|稲荷山鉄剣銘]]、[[鉄剣・鉄刀銘文|江田船山鉄刀銘]]があり、この人物画像鏡も大王号がいつ頃から使われたのかを知る手懸かりになるものである。また、いつヤマトの王が大王と称されるようになったかを解明する手懸かりになるものの一つとして注目される。
隅田八幡神社の人物画像鏡は青銅製で径19.9cm。近世の地誌類にもこの鏡についての記載がある<ref>[[1838年]]（天保9年）刊『[[紀伊国名所図会]]』が文献における初見（森岡隆 p.271）。</ref>ことから、古い時代に出土したものであることは確かだが、正確な出土年代や出土地は定かでない。鏡背は円形の鈕を中心に、内区には古代中国の伝説上の人物である東王父・西王母（とうおうふ・せいおうぼ）など9名の人物を表し、その周囲には半円形と方形からなる文様帯、その外側には[[鋸歯文]]（きょしもん）を表し、周縁部には漢字48字からなる銘を左回りに鋳出する。
この鏡の原鏡（母鏡）となった画像鏡は、[[大阪府]][[八尾市]]の[[郡川西塚古墳]]、同[[藤井寺市]]の[[長持山古墳]]、[[京都府]][[京田辺市]]のトツカ古墳、[[福井県]][[若狭町]]の[[西塚古墳]]、東京都[[狛江市]]の[[亀塚古墳 (東京都狛江市)|亀塚古墳]]などで、同型鏡または踏み返し鏡が知られている。しかし、手本となった鏡と本鏡とでは、東王父、西王母を中心として描かれた文様がすべてが逆に鋳造されている。
== 銘文と読み下し == {{quotation|[[癸未]]年八月日十大王年男弟王在意柴沙加宮時斯麻念長寿遣[[開中費直穢人]][[今州利]]二人等取白上同二百旱作此竟}} （大意）癸未（きび、みずのとひつじ）の年八月 日十大王の年、男弟王が意柴沙加（おしさか）の宮におられる時、斯麻が長寿を念じて開中費直（かわちのあたい）、穢人（漢人）[[今州利]]の二人らを遣わして白上同（真新しい上質の銅）二百旱をもってこの鏡を作る。
== 銘文の解釈 == 「大王」の「大」、「男弟王」の「男」など、必ずしも釈読の定まらない文字が多く、銘文の内容についても異説が多い。また「癸未年」がいつに当たるかについては多くの説があるが、西暦[[443年]]とする説、[[503年]]とする説が有力である。いずれも、斯麻（しま）、今州利はそれぞれ人名と解釈されている。
=== 443年説 === *[[倭の五王|倭王済]]が宋に使いを遣わして「[[安東将軍]][[倭国]]王」の称号を得た年であるから、大王は、[[允恭天皇]]を指すものと解釈する。また、意柴沙加宮（おしさかのみや））は皇后・忍坂大中姫（おしさかのおおなかつひめ、[[雄略天皇]]母）の宮処となる。この場合、男弟王は誰であるかまだ分かっていない。 *「日十大王」を「日下大王」と読み替え[[大草香皇子]]（454年没）のことではないかとする説もある（万葉集の註に「日下」を「早」と書いた例があることから「日十」を「早」の一文字とみた[[神田秀夫]]の説。のちに[[森浩一]]は「日十」は「草」の[[簡体字]]とした）。 *ちなみに古事記、上宮記によれば[[忍坂大中姫]]に[[意富富杼王]](おおほどのおおきみ)という兄弟がいるとされる。 *継体天皇の諱は「[[上宮記|乎富等]]、[[古事記|袁本杼]]（ヲ'''ホド'''）」であり、[[ハ行転呼]]以前の「男弟（ヲ'''オト'''）」とは一致しない。
=== 503年説 === *「日十大王」をヲシ大王と読んで『書紀』がその名を「大石」「大脚」とする[[仁賢天皇]]であるとし、孚弟王をフト王あるいはホド王と訓じ、「袁本杼（おほど）」「彦太（びこふと）」とある[[継体天皇]]に比定する説がある。（[[平野邦雄]]「継体朝の諸問題」「大化前代政治過程の研究」吉川弘文館 1985年、[[山尾幸久]]「日本古代王権形成史論」岩波書店 1983年）<ref>「継体天皇と即位の謎」新装版 吉川弘文館 2020‐3‐10 34頁</ref>。 *山尾幸久は「日大十王」が仁賢天皇、「男（孚）弟王」が「男大迹王」すなわち[[継体天皇]]、「斯麻王」が百済の[[武寧王]]であるとしている{{Sfn|水谷千秋|2013|p=199}}。 *[[諱]]に「'''斯麻'''」を持つ[[百済]]の[[武寧王]]（在位:502年 - 523年）とする解釈が有力である。百済は当時[[倭国]]と緊密な外交関係をもち、大陸の文物を大量に輸出しており、鏡の作者「斯麻」を武寧王と推定する。＊1971年に発見された百済武寧王の墓誌にも「斯麻王」と確認されている<ref>「継体天皇と即位の謎」新装版 吉川弘文館 2020‐3‐10 34‐35頁</ref>。 *男弟王を即位前の継体天皇のことだと解釈する。{{要出典範囲|しかし、『日本書紀』に見える「磐余玉穂宮」（526年遷宮）の前に「忍坂宮」のある大和国に入っていたこととなり記述と矛盾し、即位後19年間大和に入れなかったとする説は成り立たなくなる|date=2024年11月}}。 *{{要出典範囲|また、日本書紀における歴代天皇の在位年代を西暦に置き換えると仁賢天皇の崩御年は498年で銅鏡に刻印された日付は仁賢から皇位を継承した武烈天皇の治世となる|date=2024年11月}}。 *即位前の継体天皇は大王の後継者として[[ヤマト王権|大和政権]]の中枢部におり大和忍坂に拠点をかまえて対百済外交を担当していたことになる。[[雄略天皇]]没後、顕宗、仁賢系の王統と継体系の[[王統]]（[[皇統]]）が並立していた可能性があり、その後、継体と[[手白香皇女]]、安閑と[[春日山田皇女]]、宣化と[[橘仲皇女]]の婚姻により両王統が統合を果たしていった過程が見られる<ref>「継体天皇と即位の謎」新装版 吉川弘文館 2020‐3‐10 35‐37頁</ref>。 *{{要出典範囲|文中に百済との関係を示す語が無い事や倭国への献上品と見なすには品質が粗雑な事から、銅鏡は日本国内で製作されたものと考え、それを命じた｢斯麻｣を武寧王ではなく『書紀』に記された仁賢天皇の別名｢嶋稚子｣に比定する説もある。｢日十大王｣は｢十｣の字を｢計｣の略字と見なし、『書紀』に｢弘計(ヲケ)｣と称される[[顕宗天皇]]に比定し、｢予弟王｣は｢予の弟王｣と読んで日十大王と斯麻の続柄を示していると解釈する|date=2024年11月}}。 *近年では鏡の型式等から503年説が優勢となってきている{{Sfn|水谷千秋|2013|p=199}}。
== 脚注 == <references />
== 出典・参考文献 == * 『週刊朝日百科』「日本の国宝 40」、朝日新聞社、1997 * 森岡隆「隅田八幡神社人物画像鏡」（[[書学書道史学会]]編『日本・中国・朝鮮 書道史年表事典』、[[萱原書房]]、新版2007年（初版2005年））ISBN 978-4-86012-011-5 * 大橋信弥「継体天皇と即位の謎」新装版 吉川弘文館 2020‐3‐10 * {{Citation|和書|author=水谷千秋|date=2013-7-20|title=継体天皇と朝鮮半島の謎|publisher=文藝春秋|series=文春新書|ref={{SfnRef|水谷千秋|2013}} }}
== 関連項目 == * [[倭の五王]] * [[鉄剣・鉄刀銘文|稲荷山鉄剣銘]] * [[銀象嵌銘大刀]]

[[ファイル:Sumida Hatiman Mirror.JPG|thumb|right|隅田八幡神社人物画像鏡]] [[ファイル:隅田八幡神社人物画像鏡 (複製).JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|複製鏡}}{{small|[[和歌山県立紀伊風土記の丘]]企画展示時に撮影。}}]] '''隅田八幡神社人物画像鏡'''（すだはちまんじんじゃじんぶつがぞうきょう）は、[[和歌山県]][[橋本市]]の[[隅田八幡神社]]が所蔵する、[[5世紀]]から[[6世紀]]頃製作の[[銅鏡]]。鏡背の48字の[[金石文]]は、日本古代史、考古学、日本語学における貴重な資料である。[[国宝]]に指定されている<ref>国宝指定名称は「人物画'''象'''鏡」である。鏡は[[東京国立博物館]]に寄託されている。</ref>。

古代日本において[[大王 (ヤマト王権)|大王]]号を記す[[金石文]]としては[[鉄剣・鉄刀銘文|稲荷山鉄剣銘]]、[[鉄剣・鉄刀銘文|江田船山鉄刀銘]]があり、この人物画像鏡も大王号がいつ頃から使われたのかを知る手懸かりになるものである。また、いつヤマトの王が大王と称されるようになったかを解明する手懸かりになるものの一つとして注目される。
隅田八幡神社の人物画像鏡は青銅製で径19.9cm。近世の地誌類にもこの鏡についての記載がある<ref>[[1838年]]（天保9年）刊『[[紀伊国名所図会]]』が文献における初見（森岡隆 p.271）。</ref>ことから、古い時代に出土したものであることは確かだが、正確な出土年代や出土地は定かでない。鏡背は円形の鈕を中心に、内区には古代中国の伝説上の人物である東王父・西王母（とうおうふ・せいおうぼ）など9名の人物を表し、その周囲には半円形と方形からなる文様帯、その外側には[[鋸歯文]]（きょしもん）を表し、周縁部には漢字48字からなる銘を左回りに鋳出する。
この鏡の原鏡（母鏡）となった画像鏡は、[[大阪府]][[八尾市]]の[[郡川西塚古墳]]、同[[藤井寺市]]の[[長持山古墳]]、[[京都府]][[京田辺市]]のトツカ古墳、[[福井県]][[若狭町]]の[[西塚古墳]]、東京都[[狛江市]]の[[亀塚古墳 (東京都狛江市)|亀塚古墳]]などで、同型鏡または踏み返し鏡が知られている。しかし、手本となった鏡と本鏡とでは、東王父、西王母を中心として描かれた文様がすべてが逆に鋳造されている。
== 銘文と読み下し == {{quotation|[[癸未]]年八月日十大王年男弟王在意柴沙加宮時斯麻念長寿遣[[開中費直穢人]][[今州利]]二人等取白上同二百旱作此竟}} （大意）癸未（きび、みずのとひつじ）の年八月 日十大王の年、男弟王が意柴沙加（おしさか）の宮におられる時、斯麻が長寿を念じて開中費直（かわちのあたい）、穢人（漢人）[[今州利]]の二人らを遣わして白上同（真新しい上質の銅）二百旱をもってこの鏡を作る。
== 銘文の解釈 == 「大王」の「大」、「男弟王」の「男」など、必ずしも釈読の定まらない文字が多く、銘文の内容についても異説が多い。また「癸未年」がいつに当たるかについては多くの説があるが、西暦[[443年]]とする説、[[503年]]とする説が有力である。いずれも、斯麻（しま）、今州利はそれぞれ人名と解釈されている。
=== 443年説 === *[[倭の五王|倭王済]]が宋に使いを遣わして「[[安東将軍]][[倭国]]王」の称号を得た年であるから、大王は、[[允恭天皇]]を指すものと解釈する。また、意柴沙加宮（おしさかのみや））は皇后・忍坂大中姫（おしさかのおおなかつひめ、[[雄略天皇]]母）の宮処となる。この場合、男弟王は誰であるかまだ分かっていない。 *「日十大王」を「日下大王」と読み替え[[大草香皇子]]（454年没）のことではないかとする説もある（万葉集の註に「日下」を「早」と書いた例があることから「日十」を「早」の一文字とみた[[神田秀夫]]の説。のちに[[森浩一]]は「日十」は「草」の[[簡体字]]とした）。 *ちなみに古事記、上宮記によれば[[忍坂大中姫]]に[[意富富杼王]](おおほどのおおきみ)という兄弟がいるとされる。 *継体天皇の諱は「[[上宮記|乎富等]]、[[古事記|袁本杼]]（ヲ'''ホド'''）」であり、[[ハ行転呼]]以前の「男弟（ヲ'''オト'''）」とは一致しない。
=== 503年説 === *「日十大王」をヲシ大王と読んで『書紀』がその名を「大石」「大脚」とする[[仁賢天皇]]であるとし、孚弟王をフト王あるいはホド王と訓じ、「袁本杼（おほど）」「彦太（びこふと）」とある[[継体天皇]]に比定する説がある。（[[平野邦雄]]「継体朝の諸問題」「大化前代政治過程の研究」吉川弘文館 1985年、[[山尾幸久]]「日本古代王権形成史論」岩波書店 1983年）<ref>「継体天皇と即位の謎」新装版 吉川弘文館 2020‐3‐10 34頁</ref>。 *山尾幸久は「日大十王」が仁賢天皇、「男（孚）弟王」が「男大迹王」すなわち[[継体天皇]]、「斯麻王」が百済の[[武寧王]]であるとしている{{Sfn|水谷千秋|2013|p=199}}。 *[[諱]]に「'''斯麻'''」を持つ[[百済]]の[[武寧王]]（在位:502年 - 523年）とする解釈が有力である。百済は当時[[倭国]]と緊密な外交関係をもち、大陸の文物を大量に輸出しており、鏡の作者「斯麻」を武寧王と推定する。＊1971年に発見された百済武寧王の墓誌にも「斯麻王」と確認されている<ref>「継体天皇と即位の謎」新装版 吉川弘文館 2020‐3‐10 34‐35頁</ref>。 *男弟王を即位前の継体天皇のことだと解釈する。{{要出典範囲|しかし、『日本書紀』に見える「磐余玉穂宮」（526年遷宮）の前に「忍坂宮」のある大和国に入っていたこととなり記述と矛盾し、即位後19年間大和に入れなかったとする説は成り立たなくなる|date=2024年11月}}。 *{{要出典範囲|また、日本書紀における歴代天皇の在位年代を西暦に置き換えると仁賢天皇の崩御年は498年で銅鏡に刻印された日付は仁賢から皇位を継承した武烈天皇の治世となる|date=2024年11月}}。 *即位前の継体天皇は大王の後継者として[[ヤマト王権|大和政権]]の中枢部におり大和忍坂に拠点をかまえて対百済外交を担当していたことになる。[[雄略天皇]]没後、顕宗、仁賢系の王統と継体系の[[王統]]（[[皇統]]）が並立していた可能性があり、その後、継体と[[手白香皇女]]、安閑と[[春日山田皇女]]、宣化と[[橘仲皇女]]の婚姻により両王統が統合を果たしていった過程が見られる<ref>「継体天皇と即位の謎」新装版 吉川弘文館 2020‐3‐10 35‐37頁</ref>。 *{{要出典範囲|文中に百済との関係を示す語が無い事や倭国への献上品と見なすには品質が粗雑な事から、銅鏡は日本国内で製作されたものと考え、それを命じた｢斯麻｣を武寧王ではなく『書紀』に記された仁賢天皇の別名｢嶋稚子｣に比定する説もある。｢日十大王｣は｢十｣の字を｢計｣の略字と見なし、『書紀』に｢弘計(ヲケ)｣と称される[[顕宗天皇]]に比定し、｢予弟王｣は｢予の弟王｣と読んで日十大王と斯麻の続柄を示していると解釈する|date=2024年11月}}。 *近年では鏡の型式等から503年説が優勢となってきている{{Sfn|水谷千秋|2013|p=199}}。
== 脚注 == <references />
== 出典・参考文献 == * 『週刊朝日百科』「日本の国宝 40」、朝日新聞社、1997 * 森岡隆「隅田八幡神社人物画像鏡」（[[書学書道史学会]]編『日本・中国・朝鮮 書道史年表事典』、[[萱原書房]]、新版2007年（初版2005年））ISBN 978-4-86012-011-5 * 大橋信弥「継体天皇と即位の謎」新装版 吉川弘文館 2020‐3‐10 * {{Citation|和書|author=水谷千秋|date=2013-7-20|title=継体天皇と朝鮮半島の謎|publisher=文藝春秋|series=文春新書|ref={{SfnRef|水谷千秋|2013}} }}
== 関連項目 == * [[倭の五王]] * [[鉄剣・鉄刀銘文|稲荷山鉄剣銘]] * [[銀象嵌銘大刀]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]][[en:Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]][[fr:Miroir du sanctuaire Suda Hachiman]][[ja:隅田八幡神社人物画像鏡]][[ko:스다하치만 신사 인물화상경]][[zh:隅田八幡神社人物畫像鏡]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q847274]] Wikidata name: Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Historic Site | name = Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror | native_name = 隅田八幡神社人物画像鏡 | native_language = Japanese | image = Sumida Hatiman Mirror.JPG | caption = Bronze mirror of Suda Hachiman Shrine | locmapin = | location = [[:en:Hashimoto, Wakayama|Hashimoto, Wakayama]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | area = | built = | architect = | architecture = | governing_body = | designation1 = Japan | designation1_offname = | designation1_date = | designation1_number = }} The {{Nihongo|'''Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror'''|隅田八幡神社人物画像鏡|Suda Hachiman Jinja Jinbutsu Gazō-kyō|lead=yes}} in [[:en:Hashimoto, Wakayama|Hashimoto, Wakayama]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] is a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure of Japan]]. It is a [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]] cast with 48 Chinese characters around the fifth century. It is an important artifact for the research in Japanese archaeology and [[:en:Old Japanese|Old Japanese]] language. It was found at [[Suda Hachiman Shrine]], and its earliest mention in text is in the 3rd volume of the Kii no kuni meisho zue (紀伊国名所図会; "Illustrated Guide to Places of Note in Kii Province") published in 1838.
==Inscriptions== The mirror says {{quote|{{lang|lzh|癸未年八月日十大王年男弟王在意柴沙加宮時斯麻念長寿遣開中費直穢人今州利二人等取白上同二百旱作此竟}}}} There are a few unclear points, but a tentative translation is "In the eighth month of a ''gui-wei'' year, in the reign of the great king, when the prince Wooto was at the Palace, Sima, wishing for longevity, sent two persons to make this mirror from 200 han of brand-new and fine bronze." The year gui-wei likely corresponds to 443 or 503.
According to one prevailing opinion, the prince Wooto can be identified as the prince Oohodo, a grandson of [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]] and a brother of Oshisaka-no-Oonakatsuhime ([[:en:Emperor Ingyō|Emperor Ingyō's]] consort). Another theory holds that Wooto is Ōdo-no-Ookimi ([[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]). He may be a great-grandson (or a younger brother) of the prince Oohodo. If the gui-wei year corresponds to 503, Shima is presumed [[:en:King Muryeong of Baekje|King Muryeong of Baekje]], whose name is recorded on [[:en:Tomb of King Muryeong|his tomb]] as {{lang|lzh|斯摩}}: pronounced ''Sama'' in Korean, and ''Shima'' in Japanese.
On the basis of this ancient inscription, Korean scholar Kim Woon-Hoe theorizes a fraternal relationship between [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai of Japan]] and [[:en:King Muryeong of Baekje|King Muryeong of Baekje]].<ref name=kim>[http://www.pressian.com/article/article.asp?article_num=40081126150215&Section=04 야마토 왕조의 중시조, 게이타이천황은 무령왕의 아우] ''Pressian'' (Seoul). December 5, 2008.</ref> A mirror was excavated from the [[:en:Tomb of King Muryeong|Tomb of King Muryeong]]. It is similar to the mirrors of [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]] and [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]. In the ancient [[:en:Buyeo kingdom|Buyeo kingdom]], the bronze mirror represented the king.
== Earliest record == [[File:Depiction of the Suda Hachiman Shrine mirror in the Kii no kuni meisho zue.jpg|border|thumb|425x425px|Ink painting of the Suda Hachiman Shrine mirror as depicted in the Kii no kuni meisho zue]] The earliest record of this mirror is from the 3rd volume of the Kii no kuni meisho zue published in 1838. According to this text, the mirror had been passed down and treasured at the Suda Hachiman shrine. The monks of the shrine told the traditional account of this mirror's history. This account states that this mirror was presented to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]], after her conquest of the [[:en:Samhan|Samhan confederacies]] of Korea by "the people of that land". Although there is doubt that [[Empress Jingū#Korean Invasion|her invasion of Korea]] was a historical event. The text mentions other theories for the historical origin of the mirror, and it makes an attempt to date the artifact by analysing the script used for the inscription. The text also contains an ink painting of the mirror.
==See also== * [[:en:Seven-Branched Sword|Seven-Branched Sword]]
==Notes== <references/>
==References== * {{cite book | last = Seeley | first = Christopher | title = A history of writing in Japan | publisher = University of Hawai'i Press | date = 2000 | isbn = 0-8248-2217-X }}
{{Japan-art-stub}}

If the gui-wei year corresponds to 503, Shima is presumed [[:en:King Muryeong of Baekje|King Muryeong of Baekje]], whose name is recorded on [[:en:Tomb of King Muryeong|his tomb]] as {{lang|lzh|斯摩}}: pronounced ''Sama'' in Korean, and ''Shima'' in Japanese.

December 5, 2008.</ref> A mirror was excavated from the [[:en:Tomb of King Muryeong|Tomb of King Muryeong]]. It is similar to the mirrors of [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]] and [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]. This account states that this mirror was presented to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]], after her conquest of the [[:en:Samhan|Samhan confederacies]] of Korea by "the people of that land". Although there is doubt that [[Empress Jingū#Korean Invasion|her invasion of Korea]] was a historical event. The text also contains an ink painting of the mirror.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]][[en:Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]][[fr:Miroir du sanctuaire Suda Hachiman]][[ja:隅田八幡神社人物画像鏡]][[ko:스다하치만 신사 인물화상경]][[zh:隅田八幡神社人物畫像鏡]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q847274]] Wikidata name: Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Historic Site | name = Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror | native_name = 隅田八幡神社人物画像鏡 | native_language = Japanese | image = Sumida Hatiman Mirror.JPG | caption = Bronze mirror of Suda Hachiman Shrine | locmapin = | location = [[:en:Hashimoto, Wakayama|Hashimoto, Wakayama]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | area = | built = | architect = | architecture = | governing_body = | designation1 = Japan | designation1_offname = | designation1_date = | designation1_number = }} The {{Nihongo|'''Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror'''|隅田八幡神社人物画像鏡|Suda Hachiman Jinja Jinbutsu Gazō-kyō|lead=yes}} in [[:en:Hashimoto, Wakayama|Hashimoto, Wakayama]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] is a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure of Japan]]. It is a [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]] cast with 48 Chinese characters around the fifth century. It is an important artifact for the research in Japanese archaeology and [[:en:Old Japanese|Old Japanese]] language. It was found at [[Suda Hachiman Shrine]], and its earliest mention in text is in the 3rd volume of the Kii no kuni meisho zue (紀伊国名所図会; "Illustrated Guide to Places of Note in Kii Province") published in 1838.
==Inscriptions== The mirror says {{quote|{{lang|lzh|癸未年八月日十大王年男弟王在意柴沙加宮時斯麻念長寿遣開中費直穢人今州利二人等取白上同二百旱作此竟}}}} There are a few unclear points, but a tentative translation is "In the eighth month of a ''gui-wei'' year, in the reign of the great king, when the prince Wooto was at the Palace, Sima, wishing for longevity, sent two persons to make this mirror from 200 han of brand-new and fine bronze." The year gui-wei likely corresponds to 443 or 503.
According to one prevailing opinion, the prince Wooto can be identified as the prince Oohodo, a grandson of [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]] and a brother of Oshisaka-no-Oonakatsuhime ([[:en:Emperor Ingyō|Emperor Ingyō's]] consort). Another theory holds that Wooto is Ōdo-no-Ookimi ([[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]). He may be a great-grandson (or a younger brother) of the prince Oohodo. If the gui-wei year corresponds to 503, Shima is presumed [[:en:King Muryeong of Baekje|King Muryeong of Baekje]], whose name is recorded on [[:en:Tomb of King Muryeong|his tomb]] as {{lang|lzh|斯摩}}: pronounced ''Sama'' in Korean, and ''Shima'' in Japanese.
On the basis of this ancient inscription, Korean scholar Kim Woon-Hoe theorizes a fraternal relationship between [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai of Japan]] and [[:en:King Muryeong of Baekje|King Muryeong of Baekje]].<ref name=kim>[http://www.pressian.com/article/article.asp?article_num=40081126150215&Section=04 야마토 왕조의 중시조, 게이타이천황은 무령왕의 아우] ''Pressian'' (Seoul). December 5, 2008.</ref> A mirror was excavated from the [[:en:Tomb of King Muryeong|Tomb of King Muryeong]]. It is similar to the mirrors of [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]] and [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]. In the ancient [[:en:Buyeo kingdom|Buyeo kingdom]], the bronze mirror represented the king.
== Earliest record == [[File:Depiction of the Suda Hachiman Shrine mirror in the Kii no kuni meisho zue.jpg|border|thumb|425x425px|Ink painting of the Suda Hachiman Shrine mirror as depicted in the Kii no kuni meisho zue]] The earliest record of this mirror is from the 3rd volume of the Kii no kuni meisho zue published in 1838. According to this text, the mirror had been passed down and treasured at the Suda Hachiman shrine. The monks of the shrine told the traditional account of this mirror's history. This account states that this mirror was presented to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]], after her conquest of the [[:en:Samhan|Samhan confederacies]] of Korea by "the people of that land". Although there is doubt that [[Empress Jingū#Korean Invasion|her invasion of Korea]] was a historical event. The text mentions other theories for the historical origin of the mirror, and it makes an attempt to date the artifact by analysing the script used for the inscription. The text also contains an ink painting of the mirror.
==See also== * [[:en:Seven-Branched Sword|Seven-Branched Sword]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]][[en:Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]][[fr:Miroir du sanctuaire Suda Hachiman]][[ja:隅田八幡神社人物画像鏡]][[ko:스다하치만 신사 인물화상경]][[zh:隅田八幡神社人物畫像鏡]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q847274]] Wikidata name: Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Historic Site | name = Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror | native_name = 隅田八幡神社人物画像鏡 | native_language = Japanese | image = Sumida Hatiman Mirror.JPG | caption = Bronze mirror of Suda Hachiman Shrine | locmapin = | location = [[:en:Hashimoto, Wakayama|Hashimoto, Wakayama]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | area = | built = | architect = | architecture = | governing_body = | designation1 = Japan | designation1_offname = | designation1_date = | designation1_number = }} The {{Nihongo|'''Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror'''|隅田八幡神社人物画像鏡|Suda Hachiman Jinja Jinbutsu Gazō-kyō|lead=yes}} in [[:en:Hashimoto, Wakayama|Hashimoto, Wakayama]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] is a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure of Japan]]. It is a [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]] cast with 48 Chinese characters around the fifth century. It is an important artifact for the research in Japanese archaeology and [[:en:Old Japanese|Old Japanese]] language. It was found at [[Suda Hachiman Shrine]], and its earliest mention in text is in the 3rd volume of the Kii no kuni meisho zue (紀伊国名所図会; "Illustrated Guide to Places of Note in Kii Province") published in 1838.
==Inscriptions== The mirror says {{quote|{{lang|lzh|癸未年八月日十大王年男弟王在意柴沙加宮時斯麻念長寿遣開中費直穢人今州利二人等取白上同二百旱作此竟}}}} There are a few unclear points, but a tentative translation is "In the eighth month of a ''gui-wei'' year, in the reign of the great king, when the prince Wooto was at the Palace, Sima, wishing for longevity, sent two persons to make this mirror from 200 han of brand-new and fine bronze." The year gui-wei likely corresponds to 443 or 503.
According to one prevailing opinion, the prince Wooto can be identified as the prince Oohodo, a grandson of [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]] and a brother of Oshisaka-no-Oonakatsuhime ([[:en:Emperor Ingyō|Emperor Ingyō's]] consort). Another theory holds that Wooto is Ōdo-no-Ookimi ([[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]). He may be a great-grandson (or a younger brother) of the prince Oohodo. If the gui-wei year corresponds to 503, Shima is presumed [[:en:King Muryeong of Baekje|King Muryeong of Baekje]], whose name is recorded on [[:en:Tomb of King Muryeong|his tomb]] as {{lang|lzh|斯摩}}: pronounced ''Sama'' in Korean, and ''Shima'' in Japanese.
On the basis of this ancient inscription, Korean scholar Kim Woon-Hoe theorizes a fraternal relationship between [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai of Japan]] and [[:en:King Muryeong of Baekje|King Muryeong of Baekje]].<ref name=kim>[http://www.pressian.com/article/article.asp?article_num=40081126150215&Section=04 야마토 왕조의 중시조, 게이타이천황은 무령왕의 아우] ''Pressian'' (Seoul). December 5, 2008.</ref> A mirror was excavated from the [[:en:Tomb of King Muryeong|Tomb of King Muryeong]]. It is similar to the mirrors of [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]] and [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]. In the ancient [[:en:Buyeo kingdom|Buyeo kingdom]], the bronze mirror represented the king.
== Earliest record == [[File:Depiction of the Suda Hachiman Shrine mirror in the Kii no kuni meisho zue.jpg|border|thumb|425x425px|Ink painting of the Suda Hachiman Shrine mirror as depicted in the Kii no kuni meisho zue]] The earliest record of this mirror is from the 3rd volume of the Kii no kuni meisho zue published in 1838. According to this text, the mirror had been passed down and treasured at the Suda Hachiman shrine. The monks of the shrine told the traditional account of this mirror's history. This account states that this mirror was presented to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]], after her conquest of the [[:en:Samhan|Samhan confederacies]] of Korea by "the people of that land". Although there is doubt that [[Empress Jingū#Korean Invasion|her invasion of Korea]] was a historical event. The text mentions other theories for the historical origin of the mirror, and it makes an attempt to date the artifact by analysing the script used for the inscription. The text also contains an ink painting of the mirror.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]][[en:Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]][[fr:Miroir du sanctuaire Suda Hachiman]][[ja:隅田八幡神社人物画像鏡]][[ko:스다하치만 신사 인물화상경]][[zh:隅田八幡神社人物畫像鏡]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q847274]] Wikidata name: Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Historic Site | name = Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror | native_name = 隅田八幡神社人物画像鏡 | native_language = Japanese | image = Sumida Hatiman Mirror.JPG | caption = Bronze mirror of Suda Hachiman Shrine | locmapin = | location = [[:en:Hashimoto, Wakayama|Hashimoto, Wakayama]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | area = | built = | architect = | architecture = | governing_body = | designation1 = Japan | designation1_offname = | designation1_date = | designation1_number = }} The {{Nihongo|'''Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror'''|隅田八幡神社人物画像鏡|Suda Hachiman Jinja Jinbutsu Gazō-kyō|lead=yes}} in [[:en:Hashimoto, Wakayama|Hashimoto, Wakayama]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] is a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure of Japan]]. It is a [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]] cast with 48 Chinese characters around the fifth century. It is an important artifact for the research in Japanese archaeology and [[:en:Old Japanese|Old Japanese]] language. It was found at [[Suda Hachiman Shrine]], and its earliest mention in text is in the 3rd volume of the Kii no kuni meisho zue (紀伊国名所図会; "Illustrated Guide to Places of Note in Kii Province") published in 1838.
==Inscriptions== The mirror says {{quote|{{lang|lzh|癸未年八月日十大王年男弟王在意柴沙加宮時斯麻念長寿遣開中費直穢人今州利二人等取白上同二百旱作此竟}}}} There are a few unclear points, but a tentative translation is "In the eighth month of a ''gui-wei'' year, in the reign of the great king, when the prince Wooto was at the Palace, Sima, wishing for longevity, sent two persons to make this mirror from 200 han of brand-new and fine bronze." The year gui-wei likely corresponds to 443 or 503.
According to one prevailing opinion, the prince Wooto can be identified as the prince Oohodo, a grandson of [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]] and a brother of Oshisaka-no-Oonakatsuhime ([[:en:Emperor Ingyō|Emperor Ingyō's]] consort). Another theory holds that Wooto is Ōdo-no-Ookimi ([[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]). He may be a great-grandson (or a younger brother) of the prince Oohodo. If the gui-wei year corresponds to 503, Shima is presumed [[:en:King Muryeong of Baekje|King Muryeong of Baekje]], whose name is recorded on [[:en:Tomb of King Muryeong|his tomb]] as {{lang|lzh|斯摩}}: pronounced ''Sama'' in Korean, and ''Shima'' in Japanese.
On the basis of this ancient inscription, Korean scholar Kim Woon-Hoe theorizes a fraternal relationship between [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai of Japan]] and [[:en:King Muryeong of Baekje|King Muryeong of Baekje]].<ref name=kim>[http://www.pressian.com/article/article.asp?article_num=40081126150215&Section=04 야마토 왕조의 중시조, 게이타이천황은 무령왕의 아우] ''Pressian'' (Seoul). December 5, 2008.</ref> A mirror was excavated from the [[:en:Tomb of King Muryeong|Tomb of King Muryeong]]. It is similar to the mirrors of [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]] and [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]. In the ancient [[:en:Buyeo kingdom|Buyeo kingdom]], the bronze mirror represented the king.
== Earliest record == [[File:Depiction of the Suda Hachiman Shrine mirror in the Kii no kuni meisho zue.jpg|border|thumb|425x425px|Ink painting of the Suda Hachiman Shrine mirror as depicted in the Kii no kuni meisho zue]] The earliest record of this mirror is from the 3rd volume of the Kii no kuni meisho zue published in 1838. According to this text, the mirror had been passed down and treasured at the Suda Hachiman shrine. The monks of the shrine told the traditional account of this mirror's history. This account states that this mirror was presented to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]], after her conquest of the [[:en:Samhan|Samhan confederacies]] of Korea by "the people of that land". Although there is doubt that [[Empress Jingū#Korean Invasion|her invasion of Korea]] was a historical event. The text mentions other theories for the historical origin of the mirror, and it makes an attempt to date the artifact by analysing the script used for the inscription. The text also contains an ink painting of the mirror.
==See also== * [[:en:Seven-Branched Sword|Seven-Branched Sword]]
==Notes== <references/>
==References== * {{cite book | last = Seeley | first = Christopher | title = A history of writing in Japan | publisher = University of Hawai'i Press | date = 2000 | isbn = 0-8248-2217-X }}

December 5, 2008.</ref> A mirror was excavated from the [[:en:Tomb of King Muryeong|Tomb of King Muryeong]]. It is similar to the mirrors of [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]] and [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]. In the ancient [[:en:Buyeo kingdom|Buyeo kingdom]], the bronze mirror represented the king.
== Earliest record == [[File:Depiction of the Suda Hachiman Shrine mirror in the Kii no kuni meisho zue.jpg|border|thumb|425x425px|Ink painting of the Suda Hachiman Shrine mirror as depicted in the Kii no kuni meisho zue]] The earliest record of this mirror is from the 3rd volume of the Kii no kuni meisho zue published in 1838. On the basis of this ancient inscription, Korean scholar Kim Woon-Hoe theorizes a fraternal relationship between [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai of Japan]] and [[:en:King Muryeong of Baekje|King Muryeong of Baekje]].<ref name=kim>[http://www.pressian.com/article/article.asp?article_num=40081126150215&Section=04 야마토 왕조의 중시조, 게이타이천황은 무령왕의 아우] ''Pressian'' (Seoul). December 5, 2008.</ref> A mirror was excavated from the [[:en:Tomb of King Muryeong|Tomb of King Muryeong]]. It is similar to the mirrors of [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]] and [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]. In the ancient [[:en:Buyeo kingdom|Buyeo kingdom]], the bronze mirror represented the king.
== Earliest record == [[File:Depiction of the Suda Hachiman Shrine mirror in the Kii no kuni meisho zue.jpg|border|thumb|425x425px|Ink painting of the Suda Hachiman Shrine mirror as depicted in the Kii no kuni meisho zue]] The earliest record of this mirror is from the 3rd volume of the Kii no kuni meisho zue published in 1838. According to this text, the mirror had been passed down and treasured at the Suda Hachiman shrine. The monks of the shrine told the traditional account of this mirror's history. This account states that this mirror was presented to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]], after her conquest of the [[:en:Samhan|Samhan confederacies]] of Korea by "the people of that land". Although there is doubt that [[Empress Jingū#Korean Invasion|her invasion of Korea]] was a historical event. The text mentions other theories for the historical origin of the mirror, and it makes an attempt to date the artifact by analysing the script used for the inscription. The text also contains an ink painting of the mirror.

It is a [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]] cast with 48 Chinese characters around the fifth century. It is an important artifact for the research in Japanese archaeology and [[:en:Old Japanese|Old Japanese]] language. It was found at [[Suda Hachiman Shrine]], and its earliest mention in text is in the 3rd volume of the Kii no kuni meisho zue (紀伊国名所図会; "Illustrated Guide to Places of Note in Kii Province") published in 1838.

It is similar to the mirrors of [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]] and [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]. In the ancient [[:en:Buyeo kingdom|Buyeo kingdom]], the bronze mirror represented the king.

It was found at [[Suda Hachiman Shrine]], and its earliest mention in text is in the 3rd volume of the Kii no kuni meisho zue (紀伊国名所図会; "Illustrated Guide to Places of Note in Kii Province") published in 1838.
==Inscriptions== The mirror says {{quote|{{lang|lzh|癸未年八月日十大王年男弟王在意柴沙加宮時斯麻念長寿遣開中費直穢人今州利二人等取白上同二百旱作此竟}}}} There are a few unclear points, but a tentative translation is "In the eighth month of a ''gui-wei'' year, in the reign of the great king, when the prince Wooto was at the Palace, Sima, wishing for longevity, sent two persons to make this mirror from 200 han of brand-new and fine bronze." The year gui-wei likely corresponds to 443 or 503.
According to one prevailing opinion, the prince Wooto can be identified as the prince Oohodo, a grandson of [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]] and a brother of Oshisaka-no-Oonakatsuhime ([[:en:Emperor Ingyō|Emperor Ingyō's]] consort). Another theory holds that Wooto is Ōdo-no-Ookimi ([[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]). He may be a great-grandson (or a younger brother) of the prince Oohodo. If the gui-wei year corresponds to 503, Shima is presumed [[:en:King Muryeong of Baekje|King Muryeong of Baekje]], whose name is recorded on [[:en:Tomb of King Muryeong|his tomb]] as {{lang|lzh|斯摩}}: pronounced ''Sama'' in Korean, and ''Shima'' in Japanese.
On the basis of this ancient inscription, Korean scholar Kim Woon-Hoe theorizes a fraternal relationship between [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai of Japan]] and [[:en:King Muryeong of Baekje|King Muryeong of Baekje]].<ref name=kim>[http://www.pressian.com/article/article.asp?article_num=40081126150215&Section=04 야마토 왕조의 중시조, 게이타이천황은 무령왕의 아우] ''Pressian'' (Seoul). December 5, 2008.</ref> A mirror was excavated from the [[:en:Tomb of King Muryeong|Tomb of King Muryeong]]. It is similar to the mirrors of [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]] and [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]. In the ancient [[:en:Buyeo kingdom|Buyeo kingdom]], the bronze mirror represented the king.
== Earliest record == [[File:Depiction of the Suda Hachiman Shrine mirror in the Kii no kuni meisho zue.jpg|border|thumb|425x425px|Ink painting of the Suda Hachiman Shrine mirror as depicted in the Kii no kuni meisho zue]] The earliest record of this mirror is from the 3rd volume of the Kii no kuni meisho zue published in 1838. According to this text, the mirror had been passed down and treasured at the Suda Hachiman shrine. The monks of the shrine told the traditional account of this mirror's history. This account states that this mirror was presented to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]], after her conquest of the [[:en:Samhan|Samhan confederacies]] of Korea by "the people of that land". Although there is doubt that [[Empress Jingū#Korean Invasion|her invasion of Korea]] was a historical event. The text mentions other theories for the historical origin of the mirror, and it makes an attempt to date the artifact by analysing the script used for the inscription. The text also contains an ink painting of the mirror.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]][[en:Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]][[fr:Miroir du sanctuaire Suda Hachiman]][[ja:隅田八幡神社人物画像鏡]][[ko:스다하치만 신사 인물화상경]][[zh:隅田八幡神社人物畫像鏡]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]][[en:Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]][[fr:Miroir du sanctuaire Suda Hachiman]][[ja:隅田八幡神社人物画像鏡]][[ko:스다하치만 신사 인물화상경]][[zh:隅田八幡神社人物畫像鏡]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]][[en:Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]][[fr:Miroir du sanctuaire Suda Hachiman]][[ja:隅田八幡神社人物画像鏡]][[ko:스다하치만 신사 인물화상경]][[zh:隅田八幡神社人物畫像鏡]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q847274]] Wikidata name: Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Historic Site | name = Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror | native_name = 隅田八幡神社人物画像鏡 | native_language = Japanese | image = Sumida Hatiman Mirror.JPG | caption = Bronze mirror of Suda Hachiman Shrine | locmapin = | location = [[:en:Hashimoto, Wakayama|Hashimoto, Wakayama]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | area = | built = | architect = | architecture = | governing_body = | designation1 = Japan | designation1_offname = | designation1_date = | designation1_number = }} The {{Nihongo|'''Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror'''|隅田八幡神社人物画像鏡|Suda Hachiman Jinja Jinbutsu Gazō-kyō|lead=yes}} in [[:en:Hashimoto, Wakayama|Hashimoto, Wakayama]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] is a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure of Japan]]. It is a [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]] cast with 48 Chinese characters around the fifth century. It is an important artifact for the research in Japanese archaeology and [[:en:Old Japanese|Old Japanese]] language. It was found at [[Suda Hachiman Shrine]], and its earliest mention in text is in the 3rd volume of the Kii no kuni meisho zue (紀伊国名所図会; "Illustrated Guide to Places of Note in Kii Province") published in 1838.
==Inscriptions== The mirror says {{quote|{{lang|lzh|癸未年八月日十大王年男弟王在意柴沙加宮時斯麻念長寿遣開中費直穢人今州利二人等取白上同二百旱作此竟}}}} There are a few unclear points, but a tentative translation is "In the eighth month of a ''gui-wei'' year, in the reign of the great king, when the prince Wooto was at the Palace, Sima, wishing for longevity, sent two persons to make this mirror from 200 han of brand-new and fine bronze." The year gui-wei likely corresponds to 443 or 503.
According to one prevailing opinion, the prince Wooto can be identified as the prince Oohodo, a grandson of [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]] and a brother of Oshisaka-no-Oonakatsuhime ([[:en:Emperor Ingyō|Emperor Ingyō's]] consort). Another theory holds that Wooto is Ōdo-no-Ookimi ([[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]). He may be a great-grandson (or a younger brother) of the prince Oohodo. If the gui-wei year corresponds to 503, Shima is presumed [[:en:King Muryeong of Baekje|King Muryeong of Baekje]], whose name is recorded on [[:en:Tomb of King Muryeong|his tomb]] as {{lang|lzh|斯摩}}: pronounced ''Sama'' in Korean, and ''Shima'' in Japanese.
On the basis of this ancient inscription, Korean scholar Kim Woon-Hoe theorizes a fraternal relationship between [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai of Japan]] and [[:en:King Muryeong of Baekje|King Muryeong of Baekje]].<ref name=kim>[http://www.pressian.com/article/article.asp?article_num=40081126150215&Section=04 야마토 왕조의 중시조, 게이타이천황은 무령왕의 아우] ''Pressian'' (Seoul). December 5, 2008.</ref> A mirror was excavated from the [[:en:Tomb of King Muryeong|Tomb of King Muryeong]]. It is similar to the mirrors of [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]] and [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]. In the ancient [[:en:Buyeo kingdom|Buyeo kingdom]], the bronze mirror represented the king.
== Earliest record == [[File:Depiction of the Suda Hachiman Shrine mirror in the Kii no kuni meisho zue.jpg|border|thumb|425x425px|Ink painting of the Suda Hachiman Shrine mirror as depicted in the Kii no kuni meisho zue]] The earliest record of this mirror is from the 3rd volume of the Kii no kuni meisho zue published in 1838. According to this text, the mirror had been passed down and treasured at the Suda Hachiman shrine. The monks of the shrine told the traditional account of this mirror's history. This account states that this mirror was presented to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]], after her conquest of the [[:en:Samhan|Samhan confederacies]] of Korea by "the people of that land". Although there is doubt that [[Empress Jingū#Korean Invasion|her invasion of Korea]] was a historical event. The text mentions other theories for the historical origin of the mirror, and it makes an attempt to date the artifact by analysing the script used for the inscription. The text also contains an ink painting of the mirror.

The year gui-wei likely corresponds to 443 or 503.
According to one prevailing opinion, the prince Wooto can be identified as the prince Oohodo, a grandson of [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]] and a brother of Oshisaka-no-Oonakatsuhime ([[:en:Emperor Ingyō|Emperor Ingyō's]] consort). Another theory holds that Wooto is Ōdo-no-Ookimi ([[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]). He may be a great-grandson (or a younger brother) of the prince Oohodo. If the gui-wei year corresponds to 503, Shima is presumed [[:en:King Muryeong of Baekje|King Muryeong of Baekje]], whose name is recorded on [[:en:Tomb of King Muryeong|his tomb]] as {{lang|lzh|斯摩}}: pronounced ''Sama'' in Korean, and ''Shima'' in Japanese.
On the basis of this ancient inscription, Korean scholar Kim Woon-Hoe theorizes a fraternal relationship between [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai of Japan]] and [[:en:King Muryeong of Baekje|King Muryeong of Baekje]].<ref name=kim>[http://www.pressian.com/article/article.asp?article_num=40081126150215&Section=04 야마토 왕조의 중시조, 게이타이천황은 무령왕의 아우] ''Pressian'' (Seoul). December 5, 2008.</ref> A mirror was excavated from the [[:en:Tomb of King Muryeong|Tomb of King Muryeong]]. It is similar to the mirrors of [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]] and [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]. In the ancient [[:en:Buyeo kingdom|Buyeo kingdom]], the bronze mirror represented the king.
== Earliest record == [[File:Depiction of the Suda Hachiman Shrine mirror in the Kii no kuni meisho zue.jpg|border|thumb|425x425px|Ink painting of the Suda Hachiman Shrine mirror as depicted in the Kii no kuni meisho zue]] The earliest record of this mirror is from the 3rd volume of the Kii no kuni meisho zue published in 1838. According to this text, the mirror had been passed down and treasured at the Suda Hachiman shrine. The monks of the shrine told the traditional account of this mirror's history. This account states that this mirror was presented to [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]], after her conquest of the [[:en:Samhan|Samhan confederacies]] of Korea by "the people of that land". Although there is doubt that [[Empress Jingū#Korean Invasion|her invasion of Korea]] was a historical event. The text mentions other theories for the historical origin of the mirror, and it makes an attempt to date the artifact by analysing the script used for the inscription. The text also contains an ink painting of the mirror.

If the gui-wei year corresponds to 503, Shima is presumed [[:en:King Muryeong of Baekje|King Muryeong of Baekje]], whose name is recorded on [[:en:Tomb of King Muryeong|his tomb]] as {{lang|lzh|斯摩}}: pronounced ''Sama'' in Korean, and ''Shima'' in Japanese.

December 5, 2008.</ref> A mirror was excavated from the [[:en:Tomb of King Muryeong|Tomb of King Muryeong]]. It is similar to the mirrors of [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]] and [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]. The text mentions other theories for the historical origin of the mirror, and it makes an attempt to date the artifact by analysing the script used for the inscription. The text also contains an ink painting of the mirror.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]][[en:Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]][[fr:Miroir du sanctuaire Suda Hachiman]][[ja:隅田八幡神社人物画像鏡]][[ko:스다하치만 신사 인물화상경]][[zh:隅田八幡神社人物畫像鏡]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]][[en:Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]][[fr:Miroir du sanctuaire Suda Hachiman]][[ja:隅田八幡神社人物画像鏡]][[ko:스다하치만 신사 인물화상경]][[zh:隅田八幡神社人物畫像鏡]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]][[en:Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]][[fr:Miroir du sanctuaire Suda Hachiman]][[ja:隅田八幡神社人物画像鏡]][[ko:스다하치만 신사 인물화상경]][[zh:隅田八幡神社人物畫像鏡]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q847274]] Wikidata name: Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Historic Site | name = Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror | native_name = 隅田八幡神社人物画像鏡 | native_language = Japanese | image = Sumida Hatiman Mirror.JPG | caption = Bronze mirror of Suda Hachiman Shrine | locmapin = | location = {{ill|Hashimoto, Wakayama|en|Hashimoto, Wakayama|WD=Q842098|lt=Hashimoto, Wakayama}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | area = | built = | architect = | architecture = | governing_body = | designation1 = Japan | designation1_offname = | designation1_date = | designation1_number = }} The {{Nihongo|'''Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror'''|隅田八幡神社人物画像鏡|Suda Hachiman Jinja Jinbutsu Gazō-kyō|lead=yes}} in {{ill|Hashimoto, Wakayama|en|Hashimoto, Wakayama|WD=Q842098|lt=Hashimoto, Wakayama}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} is a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure of Japan}}. It is a {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror}} cast with 48 Chinese characters around the fifth century. It is an important artifact for the research in Japanese archaeology and {{ill|Old Japanese|en|Old Japanese|WD=Q5736700|lt=Old Japanese}} language. It was found at [[Suda Hachiman Shrine]], and its earliest mention in text is in the 3rd volume of the Kii no kuni meisho zue (紀伊国名所図会; "Illustrated Guide to Places of Note in Kii Province") published in 1838.
==Inscriptions== The mirror says {{quote|{{lang|lzh|癸未年八月日十大王年男弟王在意柴沙加宮時斯麻念長寿遣開中費直穢人今州利二人等取白上同二百旱作此竟}}}} There are a few unclear points, but a tentative translation is "In the eighth month of a ''gui-wei'' year, in the reign of the great king, when the prince Wooto was at the Palace, Sima, wishing for longevity, sent two persons to make this mirror from 200 han of brand-new and fine bronze." The year gui-wei likely corresponds to 443 or 503.
According to one prevailing opinion, the prince Wooto can be identified as the prince Oohodo, a grandson of {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin}} and a brother of Oshisaka-no-Oonakatsuhime ({{ill|Emperor Ingyō|en|Emperor Ingyō|WD=Q349258|lt=Emperor Ingyō's}} consort). Another theory holds that Wooto is Ōdo-no-Ookimi ({{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai}}). He may be a great-grandson (or a younger brother) of the prince Oohodo. If the gui-wei year corresponds to 503, Shima is presumed [[:en:King Muryeong of Baekje|King Muryeong of Baekje]], whose name is recorded on {{ill|Tomb of King Muryeong|en|Tomb of King Muryeong|WD=Q7818619|lt=his tomb}} as {{lang|lzh|斯摩}}: pronounced ''Sama'' in Korean, and ''Shima'' in Japanese.
On the basis of this ancient inscription, Korean scholar Kim Woon-Hoe theorizes a fraternal relationship between {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai of Japan}} and [[:en:King Muryeong of Baekje|King Muryeong of Baekje]].<ref name=kim>[http://www.pressian.com/article/article.asp?article_num=40081126150215&Section=04 야마토 왕조의 중시조, 게이타이천황은 무령왕의 아우] ''Pressian'' (Seoul). December 5, 2008.</ref> A mirror was excavated from the {{ill|Tomb of King Muryeong|en|Tomb of King Muryeong|WD=Q7818619|lt=Tomb of King Muryeong}}. It is similar to the mirrors of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|en|Emperor Nintoku|WD=Q313119|lt=Emperor Nintoku}} and {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai}}. In the ancient [[:en:Buyeo kingdom|Buyeo kingdom]], the bronze mirror represented the king.
== Earliest record == [[File:Depiction of the Suda Hachiman Shrine mirror in the Kii no kuni meisho zue.jpg|border|thumb|425x425px|Ink painting of the Suda Hachiman Shrine mirror as depicted in the Kii no kuni meisho zue]] The earliest record of this mirror is from the 3rd volume of the Kii no kuni meisho zue published in 1838. According to this text, the mirror had been passed down and treasured at the Suda Hachiman shrine. The monks of the shrine told the traditional account of this mirror's history. This account states that this mirror was presented to {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū}}, after her conquest of the {{ill|Samhan|en|Samhan|WD=Q1376093|lt=Samhan confederacies}} of Korea by "the people of that land". Although there is doubt that [[Empress Jingū#Korean Invasion|her invasion of Korea]] was a historical event. The text mentions other theories for the historical origin of the mirror, and it makes an attempt to date the artifact by analysing the script used for the inscription. The text also contains an ink painting of the mirror.
==See also== * {{ill|Seven-Branched Sword|en|Seven-Branched Sword|WD=Q912125|lt=Seven-Branched Sword}}
==Notes== <references/>
==References== * {{cite book | last = Seeley | first = Christopher | title = A history of writing in Japan | publisher = University of Hawai'i Press | date = 2000 | isbn = 0-8248-2217-X }}

It is a {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror}} cast with 48 Chinese characters around the fifth century. <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]][[en:Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]][[fr:Miroir du sanctuaire Suda Hachiman]][[ja:隅田八幡神社人物画像鏡]][[ko:스다하치만 신사 인물화상경]][[zh:隅田八幡神社人物畫像鏡]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]][[en:Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]][[fr:Miroir du sanctuaire Suda Hachiman]][[ja:隅田八幡神社人物画像鏡]][[ko:스다하치만 신사 인물화상경]][[zh:隅田八幡神社人物畫像鏡]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]][[en:Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]][[fr:Miroir du sanctuaire Suda Hachiman]][[ja:隅田八幡神社人物画像鏡]][[ko:스다하치만 신사 인물화상경]][[zh:隅田八幡神社人物畫像鏡]]
{{Infobox Historic Site | name = Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror | native_name = 隅田八幡神社人物画像鏡 | native_language = Japanese | image = Sumida Hatiman Mirror.JPG | caption = Bronze mirror of Suda Hachiman Shrine | locmapin = | location = {{ill|Hashimoto, Wakayama|en|Hashimoto, Wakayama|WD=Q842098|lt=Hashimoto, Wakayama}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | area = | built = | architect = | architecture = | governing_body = | designation1 = Japan | designation1_offname = | designation1_date = | designation1_number = }} The {{Nihongo|'''Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror'''|隅田八幡神社人物画像鏡|Suda Hachiman Jinja Jinbutsu Gazō-kyō|lead=yes}} in {{ill|Hashimoto, Wakayama|en|Hashimoto, Wakayama|WD=Q842098|lt=Hashimoto, Wakayama}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} is a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure of Japan}}. It is a {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror}} cast with 48 Chinese characters around the fifth century. It is an important artifact for the research in Japanese archaeology and {{ill|Old Japanese|en|Old Japanese|WD=Q5736700|lt=Old Japanese}} language. It was found at [[Suda Hachiman Shrine]], and its earliest mention in text is in the 3rd volume of the Kii no kuni meisho zue (紀伊国名所図会; "Illustrated Guide to Places of Note in Kii Province") published in 1838.
==Inscriptions== The mirror says {{quote|{{lang|lzh|癸未年八月日十大王年男弟王在意柴沙加宮時斯麻念長寿遣開中費直穢人今州利二人等取白上同二百旱作此竟}}}} There are a few unclear points, but a tentative translation is "In the eighth month of a ''gui-wei'' year, in the reign of the great king, when the prince Wooto was at the Palace, Sima, wishing for longevity, sent two persons to make this mirror from 200 han of brand-new and fine bronze." The year gui-wei likely corresponds to 443 or 503.
According to one prevailing opinion, the prince Wooto can be identified as the prince Oohodo, a grandson of {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin}} and a brother of Oshisaka-no-Oonakatsuhime ({{ill|Emperor Ingyō|en|Emperor Ingyō|WD=Q349258|lt=Emperor Ingyō's}} consort). Another theory holds that Wooto is Ōdo-no-Ookimi ({{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai}}). He may be a great-grandson (or a younger brother) of the prince Oohodo. If the gui-wei year corresponds to 503, Shima is presumed [[:en:King Muryeong of Baekje|King Muryeong of Baekje]], whose name is recorded on {{ill|Tomb of King Muryeong|en|Tomb of King Muryeong|WD=Q7818619|lt=his tomb}} as {{lang|lzh|斯摩}}: pronounced ''Sama'' in Korean, and ''Shima'' in Japanese.
On the basis of this ancient inscription, Korean scholar Kim Woon-Hoe theorizes a fraternal relationship between {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai of Japan}} and [[:en:King Muryeong of Baekje|King Muryeong of Baekje]].<ref name=kim>[http://www.pressian.com/article/article.asp?article_num=40081126150215&Section=04 야마토 왕조의 중시조, 게이타이천황은 무령왕의 아우] ''Pressian'' (Seoul). December 5, 2008.</ref> A mirror was excavated from the {{ill|Tomb of King Muryeong|en|Tomb of King Muryeong|WD=Q7818619|lt=Tomb of King Muryeong}}. It is similar to the mirrors of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|en|Emperor Nintoku|WD=Q313119|lt=Emperor Nintoku}} and {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai}}. In the ancient [[:en:Buyeo kingdom|Buyeo kingdom]], the bronze mirror represented the king.
== Earliest record == [[File:Depiction of the Suda Hachiman Shrine mirror in the Kii no kuni meisho zue.jpg|border|thumb|425x425px|Ink painting of the Suda Hachiman Shrine mirror as depicted in the Kii no kuni meisho zue]] The earliest record of this mirror is from the 3rd volume of the Kii no kuni meisho zue published in 1838. According to this text, the mirror had been passed down and treasured at the Suda Hachiman shrine. The monks of the shrine told the traditional account of this mirror's history. This account states that this mirror was presented to {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū}}, after her conquest of the {{ill|Samhan|en|Samhan|WD=Q1376093|lt=Samhan confederacies}} of Korea by "the people of that land". Although there is doubt that [[Empress Jingū#Korean Invasion|her invasion of Korea]] was a historical event. The text mentions other theories for the historical origin of the mirror, and it makes an attempt to date the artifact by analysing the script used for the inscription. The text also contains an ink painting of the mirror.
==See also== * {{ill|Seven-Branched Sword|en|Seven-Branched Sword|WD=Q912125|lt=Seven-Branched Sword}}
==Notes== <references/>
==References== * {{cite book | last = Seeley | first = Christopher | title = A history of writing in Japan | publisher = University of Hawai'i Press | date = 2000 | isbn = 0-8248-2217-X }}

{{Infobox Historic Site | name = Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror | native_name = 隅田八幡神社人物画像鏡 | native_language = Japanese | image = Sumida Hatiman Mirror.JPG | caption = Bronze mirror of Suda Hachiman Shrine | locmapin = | location = {{ill|Hashimoto, Wakayama|en|Hashimoto, Wakayama|WD=Q842098|lt=Hashimoto, Wakayama|1=Hashimoto, Wakayama}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | area = | built = | architect = | architecture = | governing_body = | designation1 = Japan | designation1_offname = | designation1_date = | designation1_number = }} The {{Nihongo|'''Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror'''|隅田八幡神社人物画像鏡|Suda Hachiman Jinja Jinbutsu Gazō-kyō|lead=yes}} in {{ill|Hashimoto, Wakayama|en|Hashimoto, Wakayama|WD=Q842098|lt=Hashimoto, Wakayama|1=Hashimoto, Wakayama}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} is a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure of Japan|1=National Treasure (Japan)}}. It is a {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}} cast with 48 Chinese characters around the fifth century. It is an important artifact for the research in Japanese archaeology and {{ill|Old Japanese|en|Old Japanese|WD=Q5736700|lt=Old Japanese|1=Old Japanese}} language. It was found at [[Suda Hachiman Shrine]], and its earliest mention in text is in the 3rd volume of the Kii no kuni meisho zue (紀伊国名所図会; "Illustrated Guide to Places of Note in Kii Province") published in 1838.
==Inscriptions== The mirror says {{quote|{{lang|lzh|癸未年八月日十大王年男弟王在意柴沙加宮時斯麻念長寿遣開中費直穢人今州利二人等取白上同二百旱作此竟}}}} There are a few unclear points, but a tentative translation is "In the eighth month of a ''gui-wei'' year, in the reign of the great king, when the prince Wooto was at the Palace, Sima, wishing for longevity, sent two persons to make this mirror from 200 han of brand-new and fine bronze." The year gui-wei likely corresponds to 443 or 503.
According to one prevailing opinion, the prince Wooto can be identified as the prince Oohodo, a grandson of {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin|1=Emperor Ōjin}} and a brother of Oshisaka-no-Oonakatsuhime ({{ill|Emperor Ingyō|en|Emperor Ingyō|WD=Q349258|lt=Emperor Ingyō's|1=Emperor Ingyō}} consort). Another theory holds that Wooto is Ōdo-no-Ookimi ({{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai|1=Emperor Keitai}}). He may be a great-grandson (or a younger brother) of the prince Oohodo. If the gui-wei year corresponds to 503, Shima is presumed [[:en:King Muryeong of Baekje|King Muryeong of Baekje]], whose name is recorded on {{ill|Tomb of King Muryeong|en|Tomb of King Muryeong|WD=Q7818619|lt=his tomb|1=Tomb of King Muryeong}} as {{lang|lzh|斯摩}}: pronounced ''Sama'' in Korean, and ''Shima'' in Japanese.
On the basis of this ancient inscription, Korean scholar Kim Woon-Hoe theorizes a fraternal relationship between {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai of Japan|1=Emperor Keitai}} and [[:en:King Muryeong of Baekje|King Muryeong of Baekje]].<ref name=kim>[http://www.pressian.com/article/article.asp?article_num=40081126150215&Section=04 야마토 왕조의 중시조, 게이타이천황은 무령왕의 아우] ''Pressian'' (Seoul). December 5, 2008.</ref> A mirror was excavated from the {{ill|Tomb of King Muryeong|en|Tomb of King Muryeong|WD=Q7818619|lt=Tomb of King Muryeong|1=Tomb of King Muryeong}}. It is similar to the mirrors of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|en|Emperor Nintoku|WD=Q313119|lt=Emperor Nintoku|1=Emperor Nintoku}} and {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai|1=Emperor Keitai}}. In the ancient [[:en:Buyeo kingdom|Buyeo kingdom]], the bronze mirror represented the king.
== Earliest record == [[File:Depiction of the Suda Hachiman Shrine mirror in the Kii no kuni meisho zue.jpg|border|thumb|425x425px|Ink painting of the Suda Hachiman Shrine mirror as depicted in the Kii no kuni meisho zue]] The earliest record of this mirror is from the 3rd volume of the Kii no kuni meisho zue published in 1838. According to this text, the mirror had been passed down and treasured at the Suda Hachiman shrine. The monks of the shrine told the traditional account of this mirror's history. This account states that this mirror was presented to {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}}, after her conquest of the {{ill|Samhan|en|Samhan|WD=Q1376093|lt=Samhan confederacies|1=Samhan}} of Korea by "the people of that land". Although there is doubt that [[Empress Jingū#Korean Invasion|her invasion of Korea]] was a historical event. The text mentions other theories for the historical origin of the mirror, and it makes an attempt to date the artifact by analysing the script used for the inscription. The text also contains an ink painting of the mirror.
==See also== * {{ill|Seven-Branched Sword|en|Seven-Branched Sword|WD=Q912125|lt=Seven-Branched Sword|1=Seven-Branched Sword}}
